segments.s642-g1: چو is a subordinating conjunction meaning 'when' here; باز آمد is a past tense verbal phrase ('returned'/'came back'), with باز as an adverb 'again/back'. از راه is a prepositional phrase 'from the road' (از + راه) that indicates source or route, and بهرامشاه is the subject/name 'Bahramshah'. Word order: temporal clause (چو + verb) + source phrase.
segments.s642-g2: به آرام is an adverbial phrase 'calmly' (به + آرام used adverbially), بنشست is the past tense verb 'sat down', and بر پیش‌گاه is a locative phrase 'upon/before the audience/court' (بر + پیش‌گاه). The verb governs the whole predicate: 'he sat down calmly in the audience.'
segments.s642-g3: ز ... اندیشه کردن is a poetic/prepositional construction meaning 'to think of/about'. The particle ز (here) introduces the topics 'مرگ' (death) and 'روز بد' (an ill/unfavorable day). تا is not present here; اندیشه is the noun 'thought/thinking' used as the object of کرد, where کرد is the past-tense verb 'did/made' completing the phrase 'he thought'.
segments.s642-g4: دلش گشت is a light-verb construction where گشت ('became') turns دلش ('his heart') into a subject that changes state: 'his heart became...'. پر before a noun means 'full (of)', so پر درد = 'full of pain/sorrow'. رخساره زرد is a nominal phrase 'face/complexion (was) yellow/pale' with the adjective زرد following the noun, giving the result clause describing his appearance.
segments.s642-g5: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning 'he ordered/said'. تا introduces a result or purpose clause ('that'/'so that'), and پیش او شد دبیر is the clause 'a scribe came before him' where پیش او ('in his presence/before him') is the locative phrase, شد is the past intransitive/happen/become verb marking the occurrence, and دبیر is the noun 'scribe/secretary' functioning as the subject that appears.
segments.s642-g6: سرافراز موبد is an appositive noun phrase 'the honored/lofty mobed (priest)'. که introduces a relative clause 'who/that', and بودش وزیر uses بود (past 'was') with the enclitic -ش referring back to the king (his), so بودش وزیر = 'who was his vizier'. The clause gives identifying information about the scribe.
segments.s642-g7: همی is a classical/adverbial particle adding continuity or emphasis ('was intending/wished'), خواست is the past tense 'wanted/intended', and تا introduces a purpose/result clause. بنگرد is a present-subjunctive form (classical prefix ب- + نگر + د) used after تا to express the intended action 'that he might look over/inspect' the object گنجها ('the treasures').
segments.s642-g8: This is a simple coordinated list of objects (زر و گوهر و جامه‌ها) followed by بشمرد, another subjunctive/potential form (ب- prefix + شمرد) expressing the intended action 'to count/number' them. The plural marker -ها on جامه‌ها marks 'garments/robes'. The verb completes the purpose introduced by تا in the previous segment.
segments.s642-g81: نهادی is the perfect/past stem of نهادن (to place/establish) used here as 'set/established'. بران is a demonstrative/adverbial 'on/upon that' (pointing back to something just mentioned). نام خراج is a noun phrase 'the name “kharāj”' (نام = 'name/call', خراج = the tax/tribute), so the whole segment means 'you assigned the name “kharāj” to that (silver)'.
segments.s642-g82: به here means 'to/for' (marks the recipient). دیوان ستاننده is a genitive/attributive sequence: دیوان = the royal court/administrative office, ستاننده = 'collector' (agentive participle) — 'the court's collector'. با governs the following nouns 'with/by means of'; فر و تاج are stock epithets meaning 'royal glory and crown'.
segments.s642-g83: به شش ماه بستد: بستد is past tense of بستن here meaning 'bound/assigned (to a period)', so 'he set it to six months' (به شش ماه marks the duration). به شش باز داد: باز here functions as 'times/turns' ('six times'), and داد is past of دادن 'gave/collected' — 'he collected (it) in six installments/turns.'
segments.s642-g84: نبودی is the past negative copula 'was not' (ن + بودی). ستاننده is again the agentive 'collector'. زان = 'from/with that' (referring back to the silver). شاد is an adjective 'happy/pleased'. The clause says the collector was not pleased with that silver.
segments.s642-g85: بدان is an adverbial/compound meaning 'by that / according to that' (ب- prefix + دان); چاره is 'remedy/plan/measure'. تا introduces a temporal/conditional clause 'until' and governs the following nominal clause مرد پیکار خون — 'a man/warrior of battle's blood' = 'a warrior sheds blood in battle'.
segments.s642-g86: نریزد is a negative jussive/subjunctive of ریختن 'to pour/leave/ shed' (here 'to shed [blood]') — 'does not / should not shed'. نباشد is a jussive/subjunctive negative of بودن 'to be' meaning 'there should not be'. به بد رهنمون is a prepositional phrase 'to (or: of) bad guidance' — i.e. 'no bad counsel/guidance should occur.'
segments.s642-g87: وزان پس is a temporal/discourse connector 'from that (and) then / thereafter'. نوشتند is third-person plural past 'they wrote/inscribed'. کارآگهان is a plural or collective noun meaning 'those informed/aware (of affairs)' — 'the knowledgeable/officials wrote.'
segments.s642-g88: که introduces a content clause 'that ...'. از here = 'from/of' and governs the nouns داد و ایمنی (justice and security). داد is the abstract noun 'justice'. وز in classical usage functions as a connective equivalent to 'and/also' (archaic), linking داد and ایمنی; ایمنی = 'security/safety'. در جهان = 'in the world'.
segments.s642-g137: کسی 'کو' بدین پایکار منست: «کسی» is the indefinite 'someone/anyone' (subject). The archaic relative/particle 'کو' here functions like classical 'که' meaning 'who/that' (introducing a relative clause). «بدین پایکار» = بدین (به + این) 'in/for this' + پایکار 'contest/struggle' — word order is preposition + demonstrative + noun. The final منست is a predicative element meaning 'is (mine/suitable/etc.)' — in context it asserts the person's relation to the contest; here the exact lexical senses provided for منست do not match its use as a copular/possessive-type predicate, so treat it as 'is (so)/belongs/is fit'.
segments.s642-g138: وگر ویژه پروردگار منست: وگر 'but/if' introduces an alternative/hypothetical continuation. «ویژه» means 'special' or 'especially' qualifying the following noun; «پروردگار» is 'the Lord/God' and is the possessor/agent in this clause. Again منست functions as the predicate ('is so'/'belongs to') with an archaic, poetic copular sense rather than the listed senses.
segments.s642-g139: کنم زنده در گور جایی که هست: کنَم is the 1st-person singular verbal form 'I will/do' (here hortatory/performative: 'I will cause'). زنده 'alive' is the object/secondary predicate. در گور 'in the grave' gives the location, and جایی که هست 'the place where he is' is a relative clause modifying the place — که introduces the relative and هست is the copula 'is'. The clause expresses 'I will (cause him to be) alive in the grave where he is' (i.e., bury him alive).
segments.s642-g140: مبادش نشیمن مبادش نشست: مبادش is an optative/negative particle meaning 'may it not be for him' or 'let it not be'. The pattern repeats for emphasis: first نشیمن 'dwelling' (place to live), then again مبادش and نشست 'seat/place to sit' — both nominal objects wishing that the grave not become his dwelling or seat. The sequence is a poetic curse formula using inversion and repetition for force.
segments.s642-g141: نهادند بر نامه‌بر مهر شاه: نهادند is the past-tense plural verb 'they placed/put/set'. بر is the preposition 'upon/on'. نامه‌بر is a compound 'letter-carrier/courier' (direct object of the prepositional action). مهر شاه 'the king's seal' is the thing set on the courier (مهر = seal; شاه = king). Word order is V + PP (they placed) + بر + recipient + instrument/mark.
segments.s642-g142: هیونی برافگند هر سو به راه: هیونی (pl./collective) 'beasts/animals' is the object/topic; برافگند is a past-tense verb 'they scattered/they drove off' (causative sense). هر سو 'every direction' (هر = every; سو = side/toward) and به راه 'along/onto the road' give direction and path. The clause means they dispersed the animals in all directions along the road.
segments.s642-g73: برین‌گونه چون نامه پیوسته شد is a temporal clause: برین‌گونه ('in this way/thus') + چون introducing 'when/once' and نامه (direct noun) followed by پیوسته شد. پیوسته here is a participial/adjectival form 'joined/finished/fastened' and شد functions as the past auxiliary ('became/was'), so the literal order is 'thus when the letter joined became' → 'when the letter was thus completed.'
segments.s642-g74: ز خون ریختن شاه دل‌خسته شد is the main result clause. ز خون ریختن is a genitive/causal phrase using the verbal noun/infinitive ریختن ('shedding/spilling') with ز 'from/because of' to mean 'from the shedding of blood.' شاه is the subject and دل‌خسته is a compound adjective 'heart-sore / weary at heart'; شد is the past copular/auxiliary 'became.'
segments.s642-g75: به هر کشوری کارداری گزید is a simple past transitive clause with a benefactive phrase up front: به هر کشوری 'for each country/realm' (prepositional phrase), کارداری (object, 'management/one who administers' — here 'an administrator') and گزید is the past tense 'he chose/selected.' Word order is typical Persian: prepositional phrase + object + verb.
segments.s642-g76: پر از داد و دانش چنانچون سزید is a descriptive tag modifying the chosen official: پر از داد و دانش 'full of justice and knowledge' (پر 'full (of)' + از marking the contents), followed by چنانچون سزید 'as was fitting/just so.' سزید is a poetic/past form of سزایدن 'to befit/deserve' used impersonally: 'as was deserved/appropriate.'
segments.s642-g77: هم از گنج بد پوشش و خوردشان is an elliptical noun phrase with an implied verb of giving/distributing: هم 'also', از گنج 'from the treasury', بد پوشش 'poor/mean coverings (cloaks)', و خوردشان a coordinated item where خوردشان (literally 'their eating') here functions as 'their food/victuals' — i.e. 'coverings and food taken from the treasury.' The actual distributing verb is omitted but understood from context.
segments.s642-g78: ز پوشیدن و باز گستردشان continues the elliptical description with two verbal nouns/verbs: ز پوشیدن 'from (their) wearing' (پوشیدن as a verbal noun/infinitive) and باز گستردشان 'and he again spread/distributed them' (گستردشان = گسترد + ـشـان, 'he spread them'). The clause is compressed/poetic: it links wearing and re-distribution, with subjects omitted as in the previous segment.
segments.s642-g79: که شش ماه دیوان بیاراستی is an explanatory/clausal phrase introduced by که 'that/because.' شش ماه is a temporal phrase 'for six months' placed before the object, دیوان 'the court/office' is the object, and بیاراستی is a past verb form meaning 'you adorned/furnished/arranged' (poetic past ending -ی). Note: the supplied sense list for بیاراستی does not include the verbal sense 'to adorn,' so the correct meaning is described here even though the provided senses do not match.
segments.s642-g80: وزان زیردستان درم خواستی continues the explanation: وزان 'and from that/from them' (here 'from those'), زیردستان 'the subordinates/vassals' is the genitive/object source, درم 'dirham(s)' is the object (money requested), and خواستی is the past tense 'you demanded/asked for.' The clause thus states that he exacted money from his subordinates.
segments.s642-g65: بران here is an adverbial/relational word meaning 'on that / from then' and heads the clause; پر خرد is a postpositive adjectival phrase (‘full of wisdom’) modifying کارها. بسته شد is a resultative/passive verbal phrase (بسته + شد) meaning ‘were closed/settled’. Word order is topic/adverbial (بران) + modifier (پر خرد کارها) + verbal predicate (بسته شد).
segments.s642-g66: ز هر کشوری is a prepositional phrase ‘from every country/land’ that sets the source; نامه is the subject/topic and پیوسته (an adverb/adjectival predicate) with شد gives a stative/result meaning ‘became/was continuous’ → idiomatically ‘letters kept coming’. The verb شد is used in the sense of ‘became/came to be’.
segments.s642-g67: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that/for'), and از here functions causally ('because of') rather than simple spatial 'from'. The three nouns داد و پیکاری و خواسته are a coordinate list of causes ('justice, fighting, and desire').
segments.s642-g68: خرد is the grammatical subject ('wisdom'); شد is the copular/resultive ‘became’; به + مغز اندرون is a locative/target phrase 'in(to) the inner brain' (به here 'towards/into'); کاسته is a past participle/adjectival predicate meaning 'diminished'. Together: 'wisdom became diminished in the inner brain.'
segments.s642-g69: ز بس is an idiomatic causal preface meaning 'because of so much'; جنگ و خون ریختن is a coordinated gerund/action phrase 'war and shedding of blood'; در جهان places the action 'in the world'. The infinitival/masdar ریختن names the action (blood being spilled).
segments.s642-g70: جوانان is the plural subject 'young people/young men'; ندانند is the present negative form 'they do not know/recognize' (classical subjunctive/negative present form); ارج مهان is the object phrase 'the honour/value of the great (مهان)'. Note Persian often omits an object marker here, so verb + object follow the subject.
segments.s642-g71: دل آگنده گردد is a resultative construction: دل (heart) + آگنده (filled) + گردد (becomes) = 'the heart becomes filled'. جوان را marks the experiencer/possessive ('the young man' as the one whose heart is affected) using the object/topic marker را. به چیز ('with things') indicates what it is filled with (literal order is more flexible in poetic Persian).
segments.s642-g72: نبیند is the negative present 'he does not see'; هم ... نیز are adverbial particles giving the sense 'even/also' surrounding the source phrase; از شاه و موبد expresses source/agent 'from the king and the mobed' (i.e. he receives/gets nothing from them). The final به is light/prepositional and in this idiom supports the sense 'also/even' together with نیز.
segments.s642-g105: سوی is a preposition meaning 'toward/to' and introduces the addressees. راستگویان and کارآگهان are plural nouns ('truth‑tellers' and 'the informed/aware people') coordinated by و; the phrase means 'to the truth‑tellers and the informed'.
segments.s642-g106: کجا is the interrogative 'where'. او is the third‑person pronoun 'he/that one'. پراگنده is a predicative adjective 'scattered/widespread' with the copula omitted (classical Persian often omits است). بد here works adverbially 'badly/ill' modifying پراگنده. در جهان is the locative 'in the world' — the whole clause asks 'where in the world is he badly scattered?'.
segments.s642-g107: که here introduces an interrogative/relative clause ('what/that'). اندر جهان is the locative 'in the world' placed before the predicate. چی + ست is the interrogative 'what is' (چی = what; ست = is), and ناسودمند is the predicate adjective 'unprofitable/useless'.
segments.s642-g108: که here introduces the consequence/relative clause 'that'. آرد is the present/simple verb 'brings' (classical narrative use). بر + این (written as برین) means 'upon/for this' (بر = upon; ین is orthographic variant of این = this), so برین پادشاهی = 'this kingship'. گزند is the object 'harm'. Word order is verb (آرد) then prepositional phrase (برین پادشاهی) then object (گزند).
segments.s642-g109: نوشتند is the past plural 'they wrote' (a straightforward narrative past). پاسخ is the object 'a reply' following the verb. که introduces the reported content. از داد شاه is a prepositional phrase 'by/from the justice of the king' (از marking the agent/cause, داد = 'justice', شاه = 'the king').
segments.s642-g110: نگردد is a negative jussive/present subjunctive form (ن‑ + گردد) meaning 'shall not be turned / shall not become' (used here impersonally). کسی literally 'anyone' is used with the negation to mean 'no one'. گرد before آیین و راه functions in this classical phrasing as 'away/from' or 'turned (away) with respect to' — together the phrase means 'no one shall be turned away from custom and the way' (آیین و راه = 'custom/rite and the way/practice').
segments.s642-g111: بشد (بَشَد) is a past/optative form meaning 'it came to be / a decision was made' (here introducing the outcome). رای و اندیشه is the compound subject 'counsel and thought/plan'. کشت و ورز are nouns 'cultivation and husbandry' describing the content of that plan (an objective to promote agriculture).
segments.s642-g112: به هر کشوری is a locative/prepositional phrase 'in/for every country'. راست here means 'right/straight' or 'set right' (qualifying the intended action), بیکار is an adjective 'idle / fallow' describing land, and مرز is 'border/frontier/land' — together the phrase indicates 'to put right the fallow borderlands of each realm' (i.e., make idle borderlands productive).
segments.s642-g25: A string of adjectives/modifiers before the noun: خنک and the coordinate phrase بی‌رنج و پرهیزگار all modify مرد. بی- is the privative prefix ('without') so بی‌رنج means 'untroubled/without suffering'; و simply links the two adjectives. This is a compact adjectival phrase describing the man.
segments.s642-g26: به ویژه is an adverbial phrase 'especially/particularly'; کسی کو بود شهریار is an archaic relative clause where کو functions like classical/poetic 'که' = 'who'. بود is the past/copular verb 'was', placing the relative clause in the past: 'someone who was a king.' Word order (کسی کو بود شهریار) is the classical order where the relative (کو) follows کسی and the copula follows it.
segments.s642-g27: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بشنید شد is a compact poetic sequence: بشنید (past 'heard') plus شد (here 'happened / came to pass'), giving the sense 'when he heard' or 'it happened that he heard.' پیش گنج means 'before/in front of the treasure' or more loosely 'to the treasure' — پیش marks direction/presence.
segments.s642-g28: به کار + infinitive شمردن forms a prepositional phrase 'to the task of counting' (به کار = 'to the work/task'); همی is the old persistent/aspectual particle indicating ongoing or customary action; برد رنج here means 'brought/caused suffering' or 'suffered hardship' — برد is the verb 'to bear/take' (here 'to incur') and رنج is its object 'hardship'.
segments.s642-g29: به سختی is an adverbial phrase 'with difficulty'; چنان روزگاری = 'such a time / such days'; ببرد is the classical/historical-present form of بردن 'he bears/undergoes' (here translated 'he endured' or 'he suffered') — poetic narration often uses this aspectual form.
segments.s642-g30: همه = 'all/everything'; پیش here means 'in the presence of / before' (i.e. reported to someone); دستور او literally 'his دستور' — دستور normally 'order/command' but in narrative contexts can imply the person in charge or 'his superior/commander' — so پیش دستور او = 'before his commander'; برشمرد is the past verb 'enumerated / recounted', so the clause means 'he enumerated everything before his commander.'
segments.s642-g31: چو = 'when'. دستور او برگرفت = 'his command/order took up' or 'when his commander adopted/took up (the matter)' — برگرفت is the past 'took up / seized / adopted'. آن شمار is the object 'that count/tally' placed after the verb for emphasis. Overall: 'When his order/commander took up that count...' (classical word order and terse poetic phrasing).
segments.s642-g32: پراندیشه is an adjective 'full of thought / pensive'; آمد is the simple past 'came'; بر + شهریار here means 'to the king' (بر used as a directional preposition 'toward/to'). The clause straightforwardly states that the thoughtful man came to the king.
segments.s642-g89: A relative/conditional clause introduced by که (that/who). هر کش is an archaic/poetic phrasing for “every/any person” (here ‘whoever’). درم بد خراجش نبود is a past negative clause: درم (a dirham/coin) + بد (adjective ‘bad/ill’ used adjectivally here) modifying خراجش (his tax/payment), and نبود is the past tense of بودن ‘to be’ (was not). Put together: “whoever had not a dirham (for) his tax.”
segments.s642-g90: An inverted existential/result clause: به سرش (into/onto his head) + اندرون داوریها (the inward/judgments within) precedes the verb فزود (increased). The preposition به here has a directional/locative sense (‘into/on’), ش is the 3rd‑person possessive clitic attached to سر, and فزود is the past form ‘increased/grew’, so: “inward judgments grew upon his head” (i.e. he grew more opinionated internally).
segments.s642-g91: ز پری (from/because of the fairy) gives the cause. نهادند is the past plural of نهادن ‘to place/put’, and its direct object is روی ‘face’ (placed on their faces). به کژی (into crookedness) indicates the kind of expression set. Word order is poetic: cause first, then the verb + object.
segments.s642-g92: پر از رنج گشتند is a compound predicate: پر از رنج ‘full of suffering’ + گشتند (they became). The following و پرخاشجوی links another resultant predicate by coordination with the earlier verb omitted: “and (they became) quarrelsome.” In classical Persian the second adjective is often left without a repeated verb and is understood to share the same verb.
segments.s642-g93: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause ‘when’. آن نامه‌بر خواند is ‘that messenger read (it)’ with خواند as the past of خواندن ‘to read’ (or ‘to recite’). The proper name بهرام گور follows as the subject affected by what was read; in narrative order the time-clause comes first.
segments.s642-g94: An idiomatic result: به دلش اندر افتاد ‘fell into his heart’—به + noun + possessive clitic (ش) gives the direction/target, اندر افتاد is the compound meaning ‘fell into / came upon’. زان کار شور literally ‘a passion from that affair’ (زان = from that; کار = affair; شور = passion), so the whole clause says that a passion arising from that matter took hold in his heart.
segments.s642-g95: ز هر کشوری (‘from each country/land’) sets the source/scope. گزید is the past of گزیدن ‘to choose/select’, so the clause means ‘he chose a مرزبانی (a border‑guard or border garrison/officer) from every land.’ Word order is subject omitted (contextual ‘he’) + object phrase + verb.
segments.s642-g96: پر از داد دلشان is adjectival: پر از داد ‘full of justice’ qualifying دلشان ‘their hearts’. چنانچون سزید is a concessive/finality phrase: چنانچون ‘just as / as was fitting’ + سزید (here passive/impersonal sense ‘befitted/was deserved’). Together: ‘their hearts were filled with justice, as was fitting.’
segments.s642-g49: بفرمود (honorific past) + پس (adverb) + تا introduces the subordinate clause of command/purpose: 'that / so that'. خراج جهان is a noun phrase 'the tribute of the realm' (خراج = tribute/payment; جهان = the realm/world). The verb that completes the clause follows in the next segment. Word order is verb — adverb — subordinating particle — object phrase (classical/poetic word order).
segments.s642-g50: نخواهند is a negative present/subjunctive 3rd‑person plural here used to express prohibition/command (they should not / they must not or 'they are not to'). نیز functions as 'also/likewise' and together with the negative gives the sense 'nor/also not'. از marks source ('from'). کهان و مهان is a coordinated noun phrase ('the ancients and the guests' / 'elders and visitors') serving as the source from which tribute must not be taken.
segments.s642-g51: به هر شهر is a prepositional phrase 'to/in each city' (به = to/into; هر = every; شهر = city). مردی پدیدار کرد is a transitive clause in past tense: 'he caused/let a man appear' (مردی = a man; پدیدار used as 'appear/manifest'; کرد = did/caused). The verb کرد is the main past verb governing the whole clause.
segments.s642-g52: سر خفته از خواب بیدار کرد is another past clause with کرد as the main verb; literal order is 'head (سر) sleeping (خفته) from sleep (از خواب) awake (بیدار) he made (کرد)'. It means 'he awoke the sleeping head from sleep' — an idiomatic way to say 'he woke those who had been asleep.' Note the postposition-like use of از before خواب ('from sleep').
segments.s642-g53: بدان تا functions as a connective meaning 'by that / so that' (ب here is the prefix and دان part of the fossilized expression); تا introduces a purpose/result clause. نجویند is negative-subjunctive/present 3rd‑person plural of جستن/juyidan 'to seek' (ن‑ prefix negates it): 'they should not seek'. پیکار 'battle' is the object; نیز adds 'also/likewise' restraint (here linking the following clause).
segments.s642-g54: نیاید is a jussive/optative form meaning 'should not come / ought not to arise' (here passive/impersonal sense 'may not come'). ز (poetic form of از) marks source 'from', followed by پیکار 'battle'. افگار is a noun/adjective meaning 'sorrowful / mourning' — the sense is 'sorrow/mourning (that might result)'. The trailing نیز repeats 'also' to emphasize that even this consequence must not occur.
segments.s642-g55: ز گنج 'from the treasure' (ز = from, گنج = treasure). آنچ is a relative/indefinite 'that which / whatever'. بایستشان is a modal form meaning 'is/was needed for them' (بایست used here as 'ought/necessary' with the enclitic شان 'for them'). خوردنی is a nominalized adjective 'things to eat / edibles' (works as a noun meaning 'food'). Together they list one category of supplies provided from the treasure.
segments.s642-g56: This continues the catalog with repeated ز 'from' before each category. پوشیدنی‌گر / پوشیدنی here functions as 'things to wear / clothing' (the compound/word in the text is a poetic form listing clothing), and گستردنی is a nominalized adjective 'things to spread / coverings, rugs, bedding' — three parallel nouns meaning 'food, clothes, and spreadables' taken from the treasury.
segments.s642-g57: بدین is a prepositional phrase formed by the preposition ب- (here 'to') attached to a demonstrative/pronoun, yielding 'to these'; پرخرد موبدان is the indirect object 'the very-wise mobeds' (مُوبَدان is the plural of موبد); داد is the simple past 'gave' and و گفت coordinates the following speech (‘and said’). Word order is typical: prepositional phrase + indirect object + verb (+ و + گفت).
segments.s642-g58: که introduces the reported content ('that'); نیک و بد is a paired noun phrase 'the good and the bad'; از من literally 'from me' indicates the person from whom something would be hidden; نباید is an impersonal modal 'must/should not' and takes the following verbal adjective/participle نهفت (from نهفتن 'to hide') to mean 'be hidden'.
segments.s642-g59: میان سخنها places 'among/the midst of the speeches' up front (میان = 'among', سخنها = 'the speeches'); میـانجی (here written as می‌انجی) is an agent noun 'mediator/intermediary'; بوید is an archaic/past copula form meaning 'was' (classical poetic form of بودن). Together: 'He was an intermediary among the speeches.'
segments.s642-g60: نخواهند is a negative/future or evaluative form ('they would not / they did not wish'); چیزی 'anything/something' is the direct object; کرانجی ('at the edge / marginally') qualifies what might be concealed; بوید here again is the archaic/past copula 'was' or a verbal form meaning 'would be', so the clause says roughly that 'they would not have anything concealed at the margins' (i.e. they hid nothing).
segments.s642-g61: مرا (written here as م + را) is the object 'me' (را is the definite/object marker attached after the pronoun); از ... آگه کنید is an instruction 'make (someone) aware of / inform about'; به و بتر here means 'the better and the worse' (i.e. good and bad) and و connects them; آگه is an adjective/noun 'aware/informed' and کنید is the 2nd-person plural/formal imperative 'do / make'. The whole is an imperative: 'Inform me about the good and the bad.'
segments.s642-g62: ز بدها 'about/from the bad things' (ز = 'from/of/about' in epic diction, بدها = 'bad things'); گمانیم is 'our/my suspicions/belief' (noun 'guman' with possessive ending); کوته کنید is an imperative 'make short / shorten' addressed to the same addressees. Together: 'Shorten my/our suspicions about the bad things.'
segments.s642-g63: پراگنده شد is a verbal phrase 'became widespread' or 'spread'; موبد is the subject 'the mōbed/priest'; اندر جهان is the locative phrase 'in the world' (اندر = 'in/within', جهان = 'the world'). Word order is subject after the verb, common in poetic diction.
segments.s642-g64: نماند here means 'remained / was left' (negated by the preceding context with ایچ 'nothing'); ایچ nika 'nothing' negates the clause; نیک و بد is 'good and bad'; اندر نهان means 'in/within hiding' — i.e. 'hidden'. Together: 'No good or bad remained hidden.'
segments.s642-g41: چو is a subordinating conjunction 'when' placed before the verb. بشنید is the simple past 3rd-person 'he heard'. بهرام is the subject name placed after the verb (poetic word order: verb before subject). و here links two actions ('and'); اندیشه کرد is a light verbal construction where the noun اندیشه + کرد (past) functions as a verb meaning 'he reflected/pondered'.
segments.s642-g42: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'/'because of'. دانش غم is a genitive sequence 'knowledge–sorrow' (sorrow that comes from knowing). نارسیده is a participial adjective 'not yet arrived / unripe' modifying غم. نخورد is a negative past form (نـ + خورد) used idiomatically here as 'did not befall/experience' rather than literal 'not eat'; its subject is the same implied 3rd-person (Bahram).
segments.s642-g43: بدو گفت is the periphrastic 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت = 'said'); کوتاه شد is a verbal phrase 'became short' (شد past of شدن) used to state that the judgment is brief; داوری is the noun 'judgment' and is the object/topic of that predication. Word order (indirect object + verb + predicate) is typical of classical poetic Persian.
segments.s642-g44: که introduces an explanatory subordinate clause 'that / for'; گیتی سه روزست is a terse equational clause 'the world is three days' (روزست = 'is [a] day' used figuratively of brevity). چون بنگری is a conditional/subjunctive clause 'if/when you look/consider' where بنگری is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive ('if you look'). Together it explains why the previous statement is true.
segments.s642-g45: چو is 'when'; دی رفت is a compact past clause 'yesterday went/has gone' (رفت past of رفتن). و links clauses; فردا نیامد هنوز = 'tomorrow has not yet come' with نیامد past negative and هنوز ('still/yet') emphasizing incompleteness. The sequence sets a temporal frame (past gone, future not arrived).
segments.s642-g46: نباشم is a first-person negative form meaning roughly 'I will not be / let me not be' (classical subjunctive/optative flavor). ز here functions 'because of'/'from' and introduces the cause: ز اندیشه امروز = 'because of thinking about today'. کوز is a concrete noun 'earthenware cup/jug' — used metonymically for present, petty concerns; the whole clause says 'I will not be (moved) by worrying over today's cup.'
segments.s642-g47: چو again gives a conditional/explanatory sense 'since/when'; بخشیدنی is a participial/adjectival form 'that will be given / to be given away' (the passive/gerundive sense of بخشیدن). باشد is the present/subjunctive/auxiliary 'is' used here to state that circumstance; و links the following nouns تاج و تخت 'crown and throne' as the things being discussed.
segments.s642-g48: نخواهم is the first-person negative prescriptive/future 'I will not want / I do not wish'. ز گیتی ازین بیش is a compact prepositional phrase 'from the world, of this any more' (ازین = 'of this' / 'from this'); رخت here means 'baggage/possessions' or more broadly 'portion/benefit'. Together: 'I will desire no more of the world's crown and throne.'
segments.s642-g121: This clause has the object pronoun 'ورا' placed before the verb phrase; 'داد باید' is a periphrastic construction (verb + modal) meaning 'must give' (older word order places the object before the verb). 'دو و چار دانگ' is a compound measurement (numeral + دانگ) reading literally as 'two and four-dang' — here 'دانگ' = a share/portion and چار دانگ is an idiom for a full share.
segments.s642-g122: 'چو' introduces a temporal clause here meaning 'when'; 'شد' is the verb 'become' (simple/past-form used idiomatically for 'becomes'); 'تا' marks a purpose/result clause and governs the negative-subjunctive/optative 'نیاید' ('so that he does not come/so that he will not cry out'). The whole tail means 'when he becomes hungry, (give) so that he won't cry out.'
segments.s642-g123: The structure 'کسی کو ندارد ...' is an older/orderly expression meaning 'someone who does not have ...'. The list 'بر و تخم و گاو' are the items the person lacks; syntactically this is a noun phrase acting as the protasis/condition for the next imperative clause.
segments.s642-g124: Imperative/prohibitive address to 'تو' (you) follows: 'با او' = 'with him', 'به تندی و زفتی' are adverbial phrases describing manner ('with harshness and with foulness'), and 'مکاو' is a prohibitive/negative-implicit form ('do not do' in an archaic shape). So the clause means 'do not be harsh and foul with him.'
segments.s642-g125: 'به خوبی نوا کن' is an imperative: 'nava kon' (literally 'make/play a tune') is used idiomatically here as 'show kindness' or 'treat kindly'. Word order has the light-verb imperative 'کن' after the noun; 'مر' (to me) appears before the object 'او را' (object marker 'را' follows the pronoun) and 'به گنج' marks direction/place 'into the treasury.'
segments.s642-g126: This is a purpose/result clause introduced by 'تا' ('so that'): 'کس ... نیاید' with final negated subjunctive 'نیاید' functions like an optative/prohibitive 'let no one come'. 'از نیستی' = 'from want/nonexistence' (i.e. from poverty), and 'به رنج' = 'into suffering/ hardship'. Together: 'so that no one from want comes into suffering.'
segments.s642-g127: 'گر' is the conditional 'if'; 'ایدونک' is an adverbial/discourse particle meaning roughly 'in this way' or 'thereby'; 'باشد' is the present/subjunctive copula 'there is' (used here in a conditional sense); 'زیان از هوا' = 'harm/loss from هوا' where 'هوا' carries the sense 'desire/whim'.
segments.s642-g128: This clause uses the negative-subjunctive 'نباشد' as an optative/prohibitive ('let there not be'); 'کسی' is the indefinite subject ('anyone/no one'); 'بر هوا' = 'under/over whim' (i.e. governed by whim), and 'پادشا' (king) is the nominal predicate or vocative — the sense: 'let no king be subject to whim.' The word order is archaic but the function is jussive/optative prohibition.
segments.s642-g113: پراگنده is a preposed adverb/adjective ('widely scattered') modifying the following verb. بینیم is the present 1st-plural form ('we see') used narratively / impersonally here. گاوان کار is the noun phrase object 'the oxen's (or cattle's) work' (گاوان = cattle/oxen, کار = work); in poetic word order the modifier and verb precede the object.
segments.s642-g114: گیا رست is a simple past clause: گیا 'vegetation, plants' is the subject and رست is the archaic past (3rd sg) 'sprang up / grew'. از دشت وز کشت‌زار is an ablative phrase 'from the plain and from the fields' — وز here functions poetically as a combination 'و ز' ('and from'), placed for metrical reasons.
segments.s642-g115: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed narrative phrase: چنین 'thus', داد past 3rd-singular 'gave', پاسخ 'answer' (direct object). The conjunction که introduces the reported clause, and تا نیم‌روز uses تا in its temporal sense 'until / as far as noon'.
segments.s642-g116: که introduces the subordinate clause. بالا کند is a verbal phrase where بالا (up/high) combines with کند (present/optative 'makes/does' here 'raises up'); the verb کند is a light causative/performative verb. تاج گیتی فروز is a noun phrase 'the crown (tāj) that illumines the world' with فروز as a postpositive epithet 'bright/illuminating'.
segments.s642-g117: نباید is an impersonal negative modal 'must not / ought not' and governs the clause that follows. کس is an indefinite subject 'anyone/no one' and آسود is a predicate adjective 'at ease/idle'. از کشت و ورز is an ablative phrase 'from cultivation and husbandry' describing what one must not be free of.
segments.s642-g118: ز (poetic form of از) marks source 'from'. بی‌ارز 'worthless' modifies مردم but is placed before it (inverted order for metrical/poetic effect). مجویید is a negative exhortation/present-tense prohibition (2nd-person plural/formal) 'do not seek'. ارز is the direct object 'worth/wealth/value'.
segments.s642-g119: که here has the causal sense 'because/for'. بی‌کار مردم = 'the people are idle' (the adjective precedes the noun, a common poetic inversion). ز بی‌دانشیست is a predicative phrase 'is from ignorance' where بی‌دانشیست contains the copular -ست ('it is ignorance' / 'due to ignorance').
segments.s642-g120: به بی دانشان = 'toward/for the ignorant' (بی دانشان = 'the ignorant', plural); بر ... گریست is the idiom 'to lament/weep over' with بر marking the object of lament. بباید is impersonal modal 'one must/ought to' and گریست is the verbal root 'to weep/lamen t' used impersonally here. Note that the lexical entry 'دانشان' as given does not match the noun 'بی‌‌دانشان' (the ignorant), so its sense is treated as not directly applicable.
segments.s642-g9: که introduces a subordinate clause ('for' / 'that'); بااو ('with him') precedes the noun ستاره‌شمر (astrologer) in a slightly archaic / poetic word order, so 'the astrologer with him had said'. گفته بود is a past perfect/periphrastic form ('had said'); بود is the past auxiliary. Together this segment means 'for the astrologer had told him'.
segments.s642-g10: ز here functions as 'because of / from' and takes گفتار ایشان ('their words/speech') as its object. برآشفته بود is a two-word predicate: برآشفته (adjective/participle 'distressed, agitated') + بود (past 'was'), so 'he was upset by their words.'
segments.s642-g11: که introduces a wished-for or reported quantity ('that/may it be'). باشد here is optative/subjunctive ('may be' / 'be'), ترا is the 2nd-person clitic written in older split form ت + را meaning 'for you', and زندگانی ('life') is the noun governed by باشد; سه بیست ('three twenties') is the numeral phrase. Literally 'that there may be for you a life of three twenties'.
segments.s642-g12: چهارم continues the numeral phrase ('and four') giving the total (i.e. 3×20+4). به مرگت uses به + noun phrase to indicate 'at/on your death', where مرگت is 'your death' (مرگ + -ت). بباید گریست is an impersonal necessity construction: بباید ('it is necessary' / 'one must') + گریست ('to weep' / 'weep'), so 'one must weep at your death.'
segments.s642-g13: همی گفت is an iterative or continuous past/present marker ('he kept saying' / 'he kept on saying'). شادی کنم uses the verb کنَم (subjunctive/first person) with شادی as its object, so 'I will celebrate / make merry.' بیست سال is a duration ('twenty years') following the verb phrase.
segments.s642-g14: که introduces the explanatory clause. دارم is first-person present 'I have / I hold'; به رفتن uses به + infinitive (رفتن) to indicate the act of going or departure ('in going' / 'as to going'); به گیتی uses به + گیتی to mean 'in the world' (here the second به has a locative/figurative sense), and همال ('porter') is a predicate/complement describing the speaker's role — 'I am a porter in the world' or 'I go as the world's porter.'
segments.s642-g15: دگر بیست is 'another twenty' (دگر = another / additional, بیست = twenty). از marks the partitive/source ('of / from'), and داد و بخشش is a coordinated noun phrase ('justice and giving' / 'gifts and charity') with جهان qualifying the domain ('to/for the world' or 'in this life'). The phrase expresses devoting another twenty (years/resources) to giving and generosity in the world.
segments.s642-g16: کنم is first-person present/subjunctive 'I will do / I shall make'. راست is an adverb/adjective 'rightly / properly', and با آشکار و نهان is a prepositional phrase 'with the open and the hidden' meaning 'both openly and secretly.' The whole segment states the manner of action: 'I will act rightly, openly and covertly.'
segments.s642-g97: به درگاه یکساله روزی بداد is a compact, poetic clause. به درگاه = “at/to the court/threshold”; یکساله is an adjectival epithet (literally “one‑year/one‑year‑old”) placed before the time adverbial; روزی here is the adverb “one day” (a temporal adverbial). بداد is the archaic/past form of دادن meaning “he gave / it happened” and serves as the main past tense verb of the clause.
segments.s642-g98: ز یزدان نیکی دهش کرد یاد is an object/clause expressing what he remembered. ز یزدان = “from/by God” (ablative/source); نیکی دهش = a noun phrase “the gift/grant of goodness” (دهش = grant/gift, نیکی qualifying it); کرد یاد = the light verb construction where کرد (did/made) + یاد (remembrance) = “he remembered.” Word order is noun phrase + light verb, a common classical Persian pattern.
segments.s642-g99: بفرمود is the honorific/polite past “he commanded/ordered.” کان را که introduces the people being spoken about (an archaic use; کان here points to those persons) with را as the definite/object/topic marker and که beginning the relative clause. ریزند is a classical present/subjunctive plural (they pour/shed) placed at the clause end, and خون is its object “blood.”
segments.s642-g100: گر آرند کژی به کار اندرون is the conditional continuation: گر = “if”; آرند is present/subjunctive plural of آوردن “they bring/introduce” used with گر to form the condition; کژی = “crookedness/wrongdoing” is the direct object; به کار اندرون = prepositional phrase “into the inner affair/household” (به marks direction/into, کار = affair/deed, اندرون = inner).
segments.s642-g101: برانند is a jussive/present plural form of راندن meaning “they should drive away / they drive away.” فرمان یزدان = “the command of God” (possessive/genitive order: فرمان + یزدان). بروی here follows the clause and functions adverbially — literally ‘on/into view’ or ‘before the face’ (i.e., openly/publicly) in this epic usage; word order places the verb earlier and the instrumental/adv phrase after the object, typical of the style.
segments.s642-g102: بدان تا شود هرکسی چاره‌جوی breaks down as a purposive clause: بدان is the fused prepositional/pivotal element (ب + دان) leading into the purpose; تا = “so that / in order that”; شود = subjunctive/passive “become / may be”; هرکسی = “everyone / whoever” (subject); چاره‌جوی = nominal compound “seeker of remedy.” Together: “by this (action), so that everyone becomes a seeker of remedy.”
segments.s642-g103: برآمد برین بر بسی روزگار is a temporal formula: برآمد = “it arose / it happened / there came about”; برین is the fused form of بر + این (thereupon / on this); بر بسی روزگار = “over many days / throughout much time” (بسی روزگار = a long time). The whole phrase thus means roughly “thereupon, after a long time” (i.e., many days passed).
segments.s642-g104: یکی نامه فرمود پس شهریار is straightforward narrative inversion common in epic diction: یکی = “one / a certain” qualifying نامه = “letter”; فرمود is the honorific past “he sent/commanded” (here ‘sent a letter’); پس شهریار = “then the king” (subject follows the verb for rhetorical effect).
segments.s642-g129: چو is the temporal 'when' (classical/epic usage). جایی is an indefinite ‘place’ (a place / somewhere) as the direct object of the time clause. بپوشد is a present/subjunctive form of پوشیدن used in narrative time-clauses introduced by چو — here translated 'cover(s)'. زمین را places 'earth/ground' as the definite object marked by را. ملخ is the subject 'the locust (swarm)'.
segments.s642-g130: برد here functions as a past/result verb 'took/carried off' (poetic narrative), followed by the object سبزی 'the green/verdure' that is removed. کشتمندان is an unusual/poetic compound here (context suggests people/fields affected) and is semantically opaque in the provided senses. به + شخ attaches an onomatopoeic particle (شخ) indicating the sound or manner: به here marks the manner ('with / by means of') and شخ is the sound-effect word.
segments.s642-g131: تو is the vocative/2nd‑person 'you'. از introduces the source 'from the treasury' (گنج = treasure). تاوان او is 'his due/compensation' (تاوان as noun, او = 'him'). بازده is an imperative/old verbal form 'give back / return' addressed to 'you' (archaic single‑word imperative rather than modern باز بده).
segments.s642-g132: به کشور means 'to/throughout the realm' (به marking direction/goal). ز here is the poetic 'by/from' and links to فرموده — 'by the (one) commanded/said (order)'. آواز ده is a compound imperative: آواز (call/announcement) + ده (give) → 'make a proclamation' or 'announce' (imperative).
segments.s642-g133: وگر is a conditional 'if / but if' introducing hypothetical alternatives. بر زمین is the locative 'on the earth'. گورگاهی (grave‑site) is the predicate nominal and بود is the past copula 'was' — together 'if there was a burial‑place on the earth'. The clause is a standard classical conditional clause.
segments.s642-g134: The second alternative repeats وگر 'and if'. نابرومند is an adjective 'ill‑fated / unlucky' modifying راهی 'a traveller' (راهی here = traveller/person on the road) and بود is again the past copula 'was' — 'or if an unlucky traveller existed.'
segments.s642-g135: که introduces a subordinate clause ('such that / so that'). ناکشته here is the negative participle 'unsown / not sown' (poetic usage). باشد is the present/subjunctive of 'to be' (classical subjunctive sense: 'may there be / be'); به گرد جهان is a locative phrase 'around the world' (گرد = around). Together the clause expresses 'so that there be no unsown (place) around the world...'.
segments.s642-g136: زمین is 'land/earth' as the noun being qualified. فرومایگان literally 'the lowly' (those of low rank) and و is the conjunction 'and'. مهان in epic contexts often means 'the great / nobles' rather than literal 'guests'; the listed senses don't capture this epic sense exactly (hence treated as irregular here). The whole phrase names both commoners and great ones as those for whom no land should remain unsown.
segments.s642-g17: نمانم is first-person negative subjunctive/optative meaning “I will not leave / I will not let (it) remain.” که introduces a subordinate clause with شود (subjunctive/passive “become”) so ویران شود گوشه‌ای = “that a corner become ruined.” Word order is poetic: main verb نمانم + که‑clause giving what he will not allow.
segments.s642-g18: بیابد is a subjunctive/optative form “may/let (someone) find”; ز من marks source “from me”; هرکسی is an indefinite subject “whoever/anyone”; توشه‌ای (indefinite with -ـی) = “some provision.” The verb precedes the subject in this inverted/poetic order.
segments.s642-g19: سوم بیست is an archaic/poetic numeral phrase (literally “three twenties,” i.e. sixty) placed before the rest of the phrase. بر پیش = “in the presence of / before”; یزدان = “God”; به پای = “at (His) feet,” a humble locative phrase. The whole prepositional string locates the speaker humbly before God.
segments.s642-g20: بباشم is an optative/subjunctive “may I be / let me be”; مگر introduces an exception “unless”; باشدم is an archaic/poetic first‑person optative/subjunctive “I may be / I should be”; رهنمای is the predicate noun “a guide.” Together: “may I be … unless I am a guide.”
segments.s642-g21: ستاره‌شمر = “the astrologer.” شست و سه is the numeral “sixty‑three” in poetic spelling/word order; گفت is the verb “said/declared.” The phrase is a simple subject + numeral + verb construction (poetic order).
segments.s642-g22: شمار = “count/tally”; سه سالش = “of those three years” (سال + possessive suffix ش); بد modifies the following verbal adjective phrase as “ill/unfavorable”; اندر نهفت = “hid/concealed within.” Word order is inverted/poetic: “he hid the count of the three years as unfavorable.”
segments.s642-g23: ز (poetic for از) = “from / because of”; گفت here = “the saying/words” (the astrologer’s utterance); ستاره‌شمر = “the astrologer”; جست + گنج = an action: جست here is the verb “sought/searched for” and گنج = “treasure.” So “from the astrologer’s words he sought treasure.”
segments.s642-g24: وگرنه = “otherwise”; نبودش is a contracted form meaning literally “there was not for him” (i.e., “he would not have had”); خود = emphatic “himself”; از گنج رنج = “suffering/trouble from treasure.” The line says that if not for that, he himself would not have suffered because of treasure.
segments.s642-g33: بدو گفت = “he said to him”: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (دِ + او > بدو) and گفت is the past‑tense verb 'said' placed after the indirect object. تا بیست و سه سال: تا here marks the temporal limit/duration 'for/until'; بیست و سه is the compound numeral 'twenty‑three' where و is the internal 'and' of Persian numerals; سال = 'years'. نیز after the numeral adds emphasis 'also/again' (i.e. 'for another twenty‑three years').
segments.s642-g34: همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily/truly'. نیازت is نیاز 'need' with the enclitic -ت 'your' (your need). نیاید is the negative form of آید used here with future/experiential force 'will not come/occur' (i.e. 'will not befall'); the negative is expressed by ن‑. به چیز = 'to/for anything' (به + چیز) meaning 'need for anything'. Word order is emphatic particle → subject/possessed noun → verb → prepositional object.
segments.s642-g35: ز ... و ز = 'from ... and from': the preposition ز (poetic from) introduces each source. خورد here denotes what fell to one's share / what was taken (an archaic/poetic noun derived from خوردن, 'that which befell/was received'), و connects the two sources, بخشش = 'gift/grant', گرفتم is the past 'I took' and شمار is the direct object 'the count/tally'. The verb follows the source phrases in normal Persian order here.
segments.s642-g36: درمهای = plural 'dirhams' (coins) and stands as the thing counted; این is the demonstrative 'this' placed before لشکر 'host/army', and نامدار is a post‑posed adjective 'renowned/illustrious' modifying لشکر. Together: 'the dirhams of this renowned army.'
segments.s642-g37: فرستاده‌ای is an indefinite/agentive form 'a messenger' (the participle used as a noun, with ـای giving an indefinite nuance). نیز = 'also'. کاید here is an archaic/poetic third‑person present/future form meaning 'will come' (parallel to آید in classical usage), and برت = بر + ت 'upon/unto you' = 'to you'. The clause thus states that a messenger will arrive to/for him.
segments.s642-g38: ز = 'from'; شاهان = 'kings' (plural). وز here represents the poetic sequence و + ز = 'and from' introducing the second source; نامور = 'renowned/famous' modifies کشورت 'your country/your realm' (کشورت = کشور + enclitic -ت 'your'). The whole phrase lists the senders: 'from the kings and from the famed (men) of your land.'
segments.s642-g39: بدین سال = 'in this year' (بدین is the prepositional/adverbial form 'in this'); گنج تو = 'your treasury/treasure' (noun + possessive enclitic -ت); آراستست is a poetic fusion of آراسته است 'is adorned / is prepared' (the perfective participle + copula 'is'), so the clause says 'your treasure is adorned/prepared in this year.'
segments.s642-g40: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'so that / for / which': پر = 'full (of)' placed before the nouns it fills; زر و سیمست = 'full of gold and silver' (سیمست = 'is silver' / 'is of silver' fused with copula, here functioning adjectivally after زر with و linking the metals). و پر خواستست = 'and full of خواست' where خواست = 'desire/request' and خواست‌ست is the poetic fusion خواست است 'is full of requests/wishes'. The copula است is often contracted to -ست in classical/poetic Persian.

sentences.s642-s1: When Bahramshah returned from the road, he sat down calmly before the court.
sentences.s642-s2: He thought about death and about an ill day; his heart became full of sorrow and his face grew pale.
sentences.s642-s3: He commanded that a scribe come before him: the honored mobed, who was his vizier.
sentences.s642-s4: He intended to inspect the treasures, to look over the gold, gems, and garments and count them.
sentences.s642-s41: You set upon that silver the name “kharāj,” to the court’s collector with glory and crown.
sentences.s642-s42: He fixed it for six months and collected it in six turns; the collector was not pleased with that silver.
sentences.s642-s43: By that measure, until a warrior spills blood in battle, there should be no bad guidance.
sentences.s642-s44: From that (time) then the knowing ones wrote that from justice and security in the world...
sentences.s642-s69: If anyone is fit for this contest, or is especially the Lord's favorite.
sentences.s642-s70: I will make him live in the grave where he is; may it not be his dwelling, may it not be his seat.
sentences.s642-s71: They set the king's seal upon the courier; they drove the animals off in every direction along the road.
sentences.s642-s37: When the letter was thus completed, the king, wearied by the shedding of blood, became disheartened.
sentences.s642-s38: He chose an administrator for each realm, full of justice and learning, as was fitting.
sentences.s642-s39: Also from the treasury (he took) poor coverings and their victuals; from (their) wearing and he again spread them.
sentences.s642-s40: For six months you furnished the court, and from those subordinates you demanded dirhams.
sentences.s642-s33: From then on the affairs of the wise were closed; letters kept coming from every land.
sentences.s642-s34: For because of justice and battle and desire, wisdom became diminished in the inner brain.
sentences.s642-s35: Because of so much war and bloodletting in the world, young people do not know the value of great ones.
sentences.s642-s36: A young man’s heart becomes filled (with cares); he sees nothing, not even from the king and the mobed.
sentences.s642-s53: To the truth‑tellers and the informed: where in the world is he scattered so ill?
sentences.s642-s54: What in the world is unprofitable — that it should bring harm upon this kingship?
sentences.s642-s55: They wrote in reply that, by the king's justice, no one should be turned away from custom and the way.
sentences.s642-s56: A resolution and plan was made for cultivation and husbandry: to set right the fallow borderlands of every country.
sentences.s642-s13: A calm, untroubled, and pious man — especially one who was a king.
sentences.s642-s14: When the treasure‑man heard, he went to the treasure; taking on the task of counting, he suffered hardship.
sentences.s642-s15: He endured such days with great difficulty; he counted everything in the presence of his commander.
sentences.s642-s16: When his command was taken up, that tally was adopted; the pensive man went to the king.
sentences.s642-s45: That whoever had no dirham for his tax grew more opinionated in his inner judgments.
sentences.s642-s46: Because of the fairy they put a crooked expression on their faces; they became full of suffering and quarrelsome.
sentences.s642-s47: When that messenger read it, a passion from that affair fell into Bahram Gur’s heart.
sentences.s642-s48: From every land he chose a border‑guard; their hearts were filled with justice, as was fitting.
sentences.s642-s25: He then ordered that they should not exact the realm’s tribute, nor take it from the ancients and the guests.
sentences.s642-s26: He made a man appear in every city; he roused the sleeping head from sleep.
sentences.s642-s27: So that they should not seek battle, nor should sorrow come from fighting.
sentences.s642-s28: From the treasure whatever they needed: food, clothing, or things to spread out.
sentences.s642-s29: He gave (them) to these very-wise mobeds and said, “The good and the bad must not be hidden from me.”
sentences.s642-s30: He acted as an intermediary amid the speeches; they would not keep anything concealed at the margins.
sentences.s642-s31: Inform me of the better and the worse; shorten my suspicions about bad things.
sentences.s642-s32: The mōbed became widespread in the world; nothing good or bad remained hidden.
sentences.s642-s21: When Bahram heard (it) and pondered, he did not suffer the sorrow that had not yet come from knowledge.
sentences.s642-s22: He said to him briefly, 'Judgment is short, for the world is but three days when you consider (it).'
sentences.s642-s23: When yesterday has gone and tomorrow has not yet come, I will not — because of thought about today’s cup — be (troubled).
sentences.s642-s24: Since it is to be given away, I will want no more crown and throne from this world.
sentences.s642-s61: Give him two- and four-dang shares (i.e. proper portions); when he becomes hungry, do so so that he does not cry out.
sentences.s642-s62: If someone has no bread, seed, or cattle, do not treat him with harshness or foulness.
sentences.s642-s63: Deal kindly with him; put him into my treasury so that no one from want comes into hardship.
sentences.s642-s64: If thereby there is harm arising from desire, let no one, O king, be ruled by whim.
sentences.s642-s57: We see the oxen widely scattered in their work; vegetation has sprung up from the plain and the cultivated fields.
sentences.s642-s58: He answered thus: 'Until midday — when the world's bright crown is raised aloft.'
sentences.s642-s59: No one should be idle from cultivation and husbandry; from worthless people do not seek wealth.
sentences.s642-s60: For the people's idleness is from ignorance; one must lament the ignorant.
sentences.s642-s5: For the astrologer had told him this, and he had been upset by their words.
sentences.s642-s6: That your life be three times twenty and four; at your death one must weep.
sentences.s642-s7: He kept saying, 'I will make merry twenty years,' for I have, in going through the world, the role of a porter.
sentences.s642-s8: I will give another twenty to acts of justice and charity for the world; I will do (it) rightly, both openly and secretly.
sentences.s642-s49: One day at the court he recalled the gracious gift of goodness from God.
sentences.s642-s50: He commanded that those who spill blood, if they introduce crookedness into the inner affairs, ...
sentences.s642-s51: They shall be driven out by God’s command openly, so that every man may seek a remedy.
sentences.s642-s52: After many days had passed, the king then sent a certain letter.
sentences.s642-s65: When locusts cover a place, they carry off the green growth with a 'shakh' (a rustling/onomatopoeic sound).
sentences.s642-s66: Give him his due from the treasure; proclaim it to the realm by (the) commanded order.
sentences.s642-s67: And if there were a grave on the earth, or if there were an ill‑fated traveller,
sentences.s642-s68: so that, around the world, there be no land left unsown for the lowly and the great.
sentences.s642-s9: I will not leave any corner to be ruined; let anyone find from me some provision.
sentences.s642-s10: May I stand before God at His feet as the sixtieth (three times twenty), unless I am made a guide.
sentences.s642-s11: The astrologer declared sixty‑three years; he hid the count of those three years as ill‑omened.
sentences.s642-s12: From the astrologer’s words he sought (or set out for) treasure; otherwise he himself would not have suffered from treasure.
sentences.s642-s17: He said to him, “Truly, for twenty-three years more you will not need anything.”
sentences.s642-s18: From the spoil and from the gifts I took the count of the dirhams of this famed host.
sentences.s642-s19: Also a messenger will come to you from the kings and from the famous (men) of your country.
sentences.s642-s20: In this year your treasury is made ready, for it is full of gold and silver and full of requests (desires).
